one_line_takeaway: ReasoningShield formalizes and empirically motivates 'CoT Moderation' as distinct from answer moderation, then trains a lightweight (1B/3B) two-stage SFT+DPO classifier on a new 7K/2.2K human-and-AI-annotated dataset that beats LlamaGuard-4 and GPT-4o at detecting harmful content hidden in LRM reasoning traces while using far less compute.
key_finding: ReasoningShield-3B achieves the highest average CoT-Moderation F1 among all tested models: 91.8% on open-source-LRM (OSS) traces and 81.4% on commercial-LRM (CSS) traces, versus LlamaGuard-4-12B's 56.2%/33.7% and GPT-4o's 76.0%/65.6% (Table 2). The abstract's claimed margins ('outperforming LlamaGuard-4 by 35.6%... GPT-4o by 15.8%') correspond to these OSS-average gaps (91.8-56.2=35.6; 91.8-76.0=15.8). The 1B variant is competitive (89.4% OSS / 77.7% CSS average F1) while using only 2.30GB GPU memory versus 135.43GB for Qwen2.5-72B, and processes samples roughly 10x faster than Qwen2.5-72B (Section 5.2/Table 7). On OOD generalization, ReasoningShield's F1 drops only ~2-4 points on unseen open-source-derived data (BeaverTails, Jailbreak subsets) relative to in-distribution AIR/SALAD subsets, and it also generalizes to Answer Moderation, outperforming WildGuard by 10.7% on WildGuard-Test despite never being trained on answer-only data. Ablations show: removing the 'evaluation guidance' component of the annotation prompt drops accuracy from 89.4 to 83.0 (F1 88.0->83.0), removing the stepwise 'analysis process' drops accuracy to 67.5 (F1 to 72.3), and substituting LlamaGuard-3's prompt drops accuracy to 82.4 (F1 79.3) (Table 3). The two-stage SFT+DPO training strategy outperforms SFT-only (CoT Moderation F1 86.3 vs 76.8; Answer Moderation F1 81.3 vs 72.5) and both vastly outperform the untuned Llama-3.2-1B base model (F1 4.2 CoT / 5.7 Answer) (Table 4).
core_contribution: The paper introduces the task of 'CoT Moderation' (also called QT Moderation) — safety classification over (query, reasoning-trace) pairs of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), as distinct from moderating final answers. It contributes: (1) a multi-level risk taxonomy (10 primary categories, 42 subcategories, 3 safety levels: Safe / Potentially Harmful / Harmful) synthesized from academic benchmarks and industry content policies; (2) the first CoT moderation dataset, ReasoningShield-Train (7,000 balanced query+CoT pairs from four open-source LRMs: DeepSeek-R1-32B, DeepSeek-R1-8B, QwQ-32B, OpenThinker-7B) and ReasoningShield-Test (2,200 human-annotated samples, 600 in-domain + 1,600 out-of-domain, spanning eight LRMs including four commercial ones — Claude-Sonnet-3.7, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Grok-3, Doubao-Seed-1.6); (3) a human-AI annotation framework using an ensemble of three judge LLMs (Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, Mistral-Small-3.1-24B-Instruct, Gemma-3-27b-it) with majority voting, reporting >92% agreement with human labels while cutting annotation cost ~97%; and (4) ReasoningShield itself, a moderation model built on Llama-3.2-1B/3B trained via a two-stage strategy: Stage 1 SFT on ~4K consensus-labeled samples to produce structured stepwise analysis + judgment, Stage 2 DPO on ~3K hard-negative (disagreement) samples to sharpen robustness on ambiguous cases.
threat_model: The paper does not model an external attacker manipulating the moderator; rather it addresses a detection/coverage gap: LRMs (o1/o3, DeepSeek-R1, Gemini-2.5, Claude, Grok, etc.) expose their intermediate reasoning traces (CoTs) to users, and because LRM training rewards are outcome-based (focused on the safety/correctness of the final answer, not the CoT), harmful content can appear in intermediate reasoning steps even when the final answer is safe (the LRM 'self-corrects' after reasoning through harmful content). Existing moderation tools were designed and trained to classify final answers, not long, exploratory, self-contradictory reasoning traces, so they miss hidden risks embedded in the CoT. The paper does briefly cite an adversarial technique from prior work (H-CoT, Kuo et al. 2025, which hijacks reasoning to drop o1/o3 refusal rate from 98% to 2%) as motivation, but this is Related Work, not something ReasoningShield itself evaluates against or defends.
limitations: The paper is currently under review (ICLR 2026 submission, not yet peer-reviewed/published) so results are self-reported and not yet externally validated. The taxonomy's 'Potentially Harmful' (Level 0.5) label is inherently subjective and the paper collapses it to 'unsafe' for binary-F1 comparison against baselines that were not designed with a 3-level scale, which could advantage or disadvantage either side depending on threshold choice. Training data (7K samples) comes from only four open-source LRMs; while the paper demonstrates generalization to four commercial LRMs at test time, base-model diversity in training remains limited. The annotation framework relies on LLM judges (Qwen2.5-72B, Mistral-Small-3.1-24B, Gemma-3-27B) for the bulk of training labels, with human correction only for the ~single-vote-disagreement subset, so systematic biases shared across these three judge models could propagate into labels un-caught by majority voting. The paper explicitly scopes out adversarial robustness of the moderator itself (e.g., whether an attacker can craft a CoT that evades ReasoningShield) and does not test ReasoningShield against adaptive attacks such as the H-CoT jailbreak it cites — this is a stated gap, not addressed empirically. Access to the ReasoningShield dataset is intentionally restricted (application required) per the paper's own ethical-considerations section, which the review notes as a genuine limitation on independent replication, not evidence the review invented.

datasets_used: ReasoningShield-Train (7,000 query+CoT pairs, built from AIR-Bench, SALAD-Bench, Alpaca queries + DeepSeek-R1-32B/8B, QwQ-32B, OpenThinker-7B traces)

ReasoningShield-Test (2,200 human-annotated samples: 600 in-domain + 1,600 OOD, built from AIR-Bench, SALAD-Bench, JailBreak-Bench, BeaverTails, TruthfulQA queries + 8 LRMs including Claude-Sonnet-3.7, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Grok-3, Doubao-Seed-1.6)
Pilot study dataset (200 questions from AIR-Bench, SALAD-Bench, JailBreak-Bench, BeaverTails -> 800 Answer pairs + 800 CoT pairs)
WildGuard-Test (300-sample subset used for Answer Moderation OOD evaluation)
BeaverTails-Test (300-sample subset used for Answer Moderation OOD evaluation)
quantitative_results: ReasoningShield-3B average F1 on CoT Moderation: 91.8% (OSS) / 81.4% (CSS) vs LlamaGuard-4-12B 56.2%/33.7% and GPT-4o 76.0%/65.6% (Table 2)
ReasoningShield-1B average F1: 89.4% (OSS) / 77.7% (CSS) using 2.30GB GPU memory vs Qwen2.5-72B's 135.43GB, ~10x faster inference

Pilot study: LlamaGuard-4 F1 drops from 44.7 (Answer) to 29.3 (CoT) on Grok-3 traces (-15.4); WildGuard F1 drops from 35.3 to 0.0 on Claude-Sonnet-3.7 CoTs (-35.3); OpenAI Moderation API F1 drops by up to 20.5 points moving from answer to CoT
ReasoningShield-3B outperforms WildGuard by 10.7% F1 on WildGuard-Test (Answer Moderation, OOD task for ReasoningShield)

Ablation on prompt components (Qwen2.5-72B as judge): full prompt Acc 89.4/F1 88.0; removing eval guidance Acc 83.0/F1 83.0; removing analysis process Acc 67.5/F1 72.3; substituting LlamaGuard-3 prompt Acc 82.4/F1 79.3 (Table 3)

Ablation on training strategy (Llama-3.2-1B): SFT+DPO CoT Acc 87.0/F1 86.3, Answer Acc 79.8/F1 81.3; SFT-only CoT Acc 77.1/F1 76.8, Answer Acc 70.0/F1 72.5; untrained base model CoT Acc 6.1/F1 4.2, Answer Acc 6.7/F1 5.7 (Table 4)

Human-AI annotation framework: >92% agreement with human labels, ~97% reduction in annotation cost; ReasoningShield-Train inter-judge Fleiss' Kappa not separately reported here but pilot-study annotator agreement Fleiss' Kappa = 0.72; ReasoningShield-Test human annotator agreement Fleiss' Kappa = 0.75
baselines_compared: Moderation APIs: Perspective API, OpenAI Moderation API
Prompted LLMs: GPT-4o, Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, Gemma-3-27b-it, Mistral-Small-3.1-24B-Instruct-2503
Fine-tuned moderation models: LlamaGuard-1, LlamaGuard-2, LlamaGuard-3, LlamaGuard-4, WildGuard, MD-Judge, BeaverDam, Aegis-Permissive, Aegis-Defensive
